title: Saving the Civil Service Act
plain_summary: This bill protects government workers from being moved to different job categories without their permission. It stops presidents from creating new job types like Schedule F that would make it easier to fire government employees. The bill says agencies can only move a small number of workers from protected jobs to unprotected jobs each presidential term. It also requires workers to agree before their jobs can be changed to a different category.
last_action_text: ASSUMING FIRST SPONSORSHIP - Mr. Walkinshaw asked unanimous consent that he may hereafter be considered as the first sponsor of H.R. 492, a bill originally introduced by Representative Connolly, for t
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Saving the Civil Service Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Limitations on Excepting Positions from Competitive Service and Transferring Positions
sections.text: This section sets rules about how government jobs can be moved between different job categories. The competitive service includes most federal government jobs where people must take tests or go through fair hiring processes. The excepted service includes special government jobs that do not require the usual competitive hiring process. A job in the competitive service can only be moved out of that category if it goes into one of the existing job schedules called A through E that were in place on September thirtieth two thousand twenty. These moves must follow the same rules that were in effect on that date. Jobs already in the excepted service cannot be moved to any schedule other than schedules A through E. Government agencies cannot move any filled job into schedule C without first getting permission from the Director of the Office of Personnel Management. Schedule C jobs are political appointments that can be filled by people chosen by elected officials. During any four year presidential term, an agency cannot move more than one percent of its total workers from competitive service to excepted service, or five employees, whichever number is higher. This limit is based on how many employees the agency had on the first day of the presidential term. No employee can be moved from one excepted service schedule to another without their written permission. No employee in competitive service can be moved to excepted service without their written permission. These rules apply to all federal government positions, including those at the Department of Veterans Affairs that are usually handled under different rules. The Director of the Office of Personnel Management must create regulations to make these rules work. In this section, agency means any department, agency, or part of the federal government. Competitive service means the job category defined in section twenty one oh two of title five of United States Code. Director means the Director of the Office of Personnel Management. Excepted service means the job category defined in section twenty one oh three of title five of United States Code.
